text: LEC 4 Hybrid Systems Introduction Cyber-physical systems integrate physical dynamics and computational systems, so they commonly combine both discrete and continuous dynamics. In this chapter, we show that the modeling techniques of Chapters 2 and 3 can be combined, yielding what are known as hybrid systems. Hybrid system models are often much simpler and more understandable. They are a powerful tool for understanding real- world systems.

Modal Models In this section, we show that state machines can be generalized to admit continuous inputs and outputs and to combine discrete and continuous dynamics. A hybrid system is sometimes called a modal model because it has a finite number of modes, one for each state of the FSM. The states of the FSM may be referred to as modes rather than states

Actor Model for State Machines It is natural to think of a state machine as an actor, as illustrated in Figure What About Continuous Inputs? We have so far assumed that state machines operate in a sequence of discrete reactions. • We have assumed that inputs and outputs are absent between reactions. We will now generalize this to allow inputs and outputs to be continuous-time signals. In order to get state machine models to coexist with time-based models, we need to interpret state transitions to occur on the same timeline used for the time-based portion of the system.

Example 1 Consider a thermostat modeled as a state machine with states = {heating; cooling}, shown in Figure. Cont., The input is a continuous-time signal Ƭ : R (cid:0) R where Ƭ(t) represents the temperature at time t. • The initial state is cooling, and the transition out of this state is enabled at the earliest time t after the start time when Ƭ(t) ≤ 18. In this example, we assume the outputs are pure signals {heatOn and heatOff}. What About Continuous Outputs? • In the above example, the outputs are present only at the times the transitions are taken. • We can also generalize FSMs to support continuous-time outputs, but to do this, we need the notion of state refinements.

A hybrid system associates with each state of an FSM a dynamic behavior, called “state refinements” that gives the dynamic behavior of the output as a function of the input and (possibly) additional continuous state variables.

Example 2 Suppose that instead of discrete outputs as in Example 1 we wish to produce a control signal whose value is 1 when the heat is on and 0 when the heat is off. Such a control signal could directly drive a heater. • Each state has a refinement that gives the value of the output h while the state machine is in that state. Classes of Hybrid Systems: 1- Timed Automata • Most cyber-physical systems require measuring the passage of time and performing actions at specific times. A device that measures the passage of time, a clock, has a particularly simple dynamics: its state progresses linearly in time. Timed automata are the simplest non-trivial hybrid systems.

They are modal models where the time-based refinements have very simple dynamics; all they do is measure the passage of time. Clock Model A clock is modeled by a first-order differential equation, Example 3 Recall the thermostat of Example 1, which uses hysteresis to prevent chattering. An alternative implementation that would also prevent chattering would use a single temperature threshold, but instead would require that the heater remain on or off for at least a minimum amount of time, regardless of the temperature. This can be modeled as a timed automaton.

Example 4 A timed automaton produces a pure output that will be present every T time units, starting at the time when the system begins executing. Example 5 The traffic light controller is a time triggered machine that assumes it reacts once each second. Sheet 4 Exercises: 2, 4, 6, and 9.
